name: Pebble engine read perf — minimize UnpackReadGrants 1M paginated read

description: Baseline for the read-perf session, starting from the autoresearch/pebble-perf-20260525 branch (WritePack session end state). pebble_readpaginated_1m_ms = 1229.6 ms. Allocs at 1M scale = 17M (versus 22K on the WritePack side — likely a big arena-like win available). SQLite + WritePack sentinels within tolerance.

asi.hypothesis: establish a clean baseline for the read-perf session
asi.allocs_at_1m_anomaly: 17M allocs at 1M scale. The bench paginates 1M grants in 100 pages of 10k each. Each grant likely triggers a few allocations (V3GrantToV2 + nested stubs); 1M × ~17 ≈ 17M matches. Strong hint that an arena-style fix applies (like WritePack session #41).
asi.next_idea: Profile-first: pprof the 1M read to localize the actual bottleneck (NewStore/unpack vs paginated iteration vs Close). Most likely the unpack (ExtractZstdTar) dominates at large scales because the c1z is ~500MB to extract.
asi.iter_time_observation: Full bench sweep runs in ~17s wallclock; much faster than the WritePack session's ~190s. Can afford more iterations per session.

description: grantReadArena for the paginated grants read path with pre-populated nested fields (Entitlement, Principal, DiscoveredAt). Hypothesis: proto.Unmarshal's 'if field == nil, allocate' check would reuse the pre-populated arena slots. Reality: proto.Unmarshal allocates fresh nested messages anyway (allocs only dropped 17.0M\u219216.0M = -5.9%, which matches just the OUTER GrantRecord arena win, not the nested fields). The pre-populated nested arenas are wasted memory \u2014 bytes_op +15.4%, smaller scales regressed +28-40% at 1k/100. Primary flat (+0.5%).

asi.hypothesis: proto.Unmarshal's generated code uses 'if nested == nil { new(T) }' pattern, so pre-populating with arena slots should let the runtime reuse them.
asi.result: Wrong about the proto runtime. Allocs dropped only by the 1M outer GrantRecord count; nested fields still allocated fresh. Smaller scales regressed because arena was sized to DefaultPageSize=10000 regardless of actual records.
asi.rollback_reason: primary flat, smaller scales clearly regressed, bytes_op up.
asi.mechanism_failure: The protobuf runtime's behavior on non-nil nested fields is more complex than the simple if-nil-allocate I assumed. Either (1) it CLEARS the existing nested field before unmarshaling, (2) it MERGES into the existing field but my pre-populated field gets discarded for some other reason, or (3) the generated unmarshal code path for v3 GrantRecord uses a different pattern. Need to read the actual unmarshal code path more carefully.
asi.next_action_hint: Two angles: (a) just arena the OUTER GrantRecord (lose only -5.9% allocs, modest win); (b) figure out HOW to actually share nested allocations across the page — maybe via proto.UnmarshalState directly bypassing the generated Unmarshal, or by writing custom decode for GrantRecord. Also: the arena should be sized to limit OR len(actual_records), not always DefaultPageSize, to avoid over-allocation at small scales.

description: Outer-only grantReadArena for PaginateGrantsBySync. Collapses per-iter v3.GrantRecord allocations from O(records) to O(pages) \u2014 for the 1M read bench, 1M outer allocs become 100 slice allocs (one per page). pebble_readpaginated_1m_ms 1229.6\u21921201.4 ms (-2.3%); pebble_readpaginated_100k_ms 129.6\u2192122.2 (-5.7% same direction confirms). Allocs/op 17.03M\u219216.03M (-5.9%, matches the eliminated outer GrantRecord allocs). Smaller scales regressed (1k +14%, 100 +20%) due to over-sized arena: pre-allocates 10 k slots but workloads of 100/1k only fill a fraction. WritePack + SQLite sentinels flat. Followup: switch to slab-style growable arena to fix small-scale regression.

asi.hypothesis: outer v3.GrantRecord allocations in iteratePrimaryPageWithKey (1M per 1M read) collapse to O(pages) via arena. Pre-populating nested fields didn't work (see #50); just arena the outer struct.
asi.result: primary -2.3% (1229.6→1201.4 ms), 100k -5.7% directionally confirms. Allocs/op dropped by exactly 1M (the outer GrantRecord count), matching the mechanism.
asi.small_scale_regression: 1k +14%, 100 +20%. Cause: arena pre-allocates DefaultPageSize=10000 slots; small workloads waste 9000+ slots = ~700 KB of memclr per call. For a 1.77 ms baseline at 100 scale, an extra 350 µs of unused-arena alloc work is significant.
asi.fix_idea_for_next_iteration: Use a slab-style arena: initial slab of 256-512 entries, grow by allocating a NEW slab (preserving pointers in earlier slabs). Avoids the upfront 10000-slot waste at small scales while keeping pointer stability for the returned page.
asi.sentinels: pebble_writepack_1m_ms 1262→1243 (within noise, no regression). sqlite_readpaginated_1k_ms 5.87→6.07 (within noise).
asi.cumulative_progress: Read session iteration 2: -2.3% from session baseline.

description: Slab-style growable arena (initial cap 256, doubling to 4 K cap). Intent: fix #51's small-scale regression by not over-allocating 10 K slots for tiny reads. Primary flat (-0.2% vs #51) and small scales slightly worse (100 +4.2%, 100k +4.4% vs #51). The slab management overhead (per-call if-len-equals-cap check + slab append + slice-header sync) outweighs the saved memclr at small scales.

asi.hypothesis: slab-style arena fixes #51's small-scale regression while keeping the 1M win
asi.result: primary flat (-0.2%), small scales slightly worse vs #51. Multiple slabs (256+512+1024+...) added per-call branch overhead that isn't compensated by the saved memclr at small scales.
asi.rollback_reason: primary didn't improve and small scales got slightly worse — the simpler fixed-size arena from #51 is the better tradeoff.
asi.lesson: When the arena's pre-allocated waste is small in absolute terms (e.g., 800 KB memclr = 80 µs), the management overhead of a growable arena can exceed the saved work. Simpler fixed-size arena with a known upper bound on page size is the right answer.

description: grantV2ReadArena: arena-allocate the 6 v2.Grant nested stubs (Grant + Entitlement + 2 \u00d7 (Resource + ResourceId)) in adapter.ListGrants. Each translateV3Grant call now allocates O(1) into the per-page arena instead of 6 individual mallocs. pebble_readpaginated_1m_ms 1229.6\u2192974.6 ms (-20.7%); pebble_readpaginated_100k_ms 129.6\u2192104.9 (-19.1% same direction confirms). Allocs/op 17.0M\u219210.0M (-41%) \u2014 exactly the 6M eliminated stub allocations matching the mechanism. bytes_op flat. SQLite + WritePack sentinels flat. Smaller scales still regress (1k +16%, 100 +28%) due to arena-allocation overhead at sizes where direct allocation was already cheap.

asi.hypothesis: V3GrantToV2 allocates 6 nested stub structs per grant (v2.Grant + Entitlement + 2 Resources + 2 ResourceIds). Per profile this was 190 ms CPU for the 1M paginated read. Arena them similar to V2GrantToV3 (WritePack session #41).
asi.result: primary -20.7% (1229.6→974.6 ms). Allocs -41% exactly matching the 6M eliminated stubs. 100k -19.1% confirms.
asi.mechanism_insight: Two compounding effects: (1) 6M individual mallocs become 6 slice allocs; (2) the per-page arena lives only as long as the response is consumed, so GC reclamation is cheap. Total alloc count dropped by exactly what was theoretically possible.
asi.cumulative_progress: Read session iteration 4: -20.7% from session baseline 1229.6 ms. Combined with #51's outer GrantRecord arena (-2.3%), session total is -20.7% from baseline.
asi.small_scale_regression_note: 1k +16% and 100 +28% — same pattern as #51's outer arena. Arena pre-allocates `len(records)` slots; for small pages this is fine, but the per-call arena struct + 6 slice headers add ~60 µs overhead that matters at the 1.77 ms / 2.93 ms scale of the 100/1k benches. Production callers usually do bulk reads, where the tradeoff is overwhelming.
asi.tradeoff_assessment: Bulk-read callers (sync engines, batch processors): -19-21% wallclock. Small-read callers: +16-28% wallclock at the smallest scales. The primary metric (1M) is the optimization target; bulk-read use case dominates production. Keep.
asi.next_action_hint: Profile again. After eliminating outer GrantRecord + v2 stubs, the remaining 974 ms wallclock has the nested proto.Unmarshal allocations (EntitlementRef, PrincipalRef, Timestamp, strings inside) still firing fresh allocations. That's 4M allocs/grant × 1M = 4M of the remaining 10M total. Hard to eliminate without going to a custom decoder.

description: Parallel proto.Unmarshal via 4-worker pool in PaginateGrantsBySync. Main goroutine iterates Pebble, copies each value's bytes (Pebble's iter.Value() invalidated on Next()) and dispatches (idx, bytes) to workers. Pre-allocated arena slots indexed by job idx avoid race conditions. Primary +10.8% REGRESSION. bytes_op +10.5% from per-record value-buffer allocations; allocs_op +1M from `make([]byte, len(v))` per record. The dispatch overhead (channel send/recv at ~50-100 ns each \u00d7 1M = 50-100 ms) and the per-record byte-buffer alloc swamped the theoretical 350 ms parallel-decode savings.

asi.hypothesis: proto.Unmarshal at 470 ms CPU is the serial bottleneck. 4-worker parallelism would cut to ~120 ms + 50 ms copy = 170 ms total decode work. Save ~300 ms wallclock.
asi.result: primary +10.8% regression. ~+105 ms wallclock. All other scales regressed too.
asi.mechanism_failure: Three compounding overheads I underestimated: (1) per-record value-buffer allocation — 1M new `make([]byte, len(v))` calls cost ~30 ns each = 30 ms PLUS the GC pressure from tracking 1M new objects. (2) channel send + receive synchronization per job — ~50 ns each × 2 (send + receive) × 1M = 100 ms. (3) goroutine wakeup latency — 4 workers pulling from one channel cause cache-line contention on the channel's internal buffer.
asi.next_attempts_if_pursued: Could mitigate (1) via a slab-buffer pool, and (2) via batched job dispatch (group N records per job). But the savings ceiling is still bounded by Amdahl's law: the iter.Next + value-copy work still has to happen serially on the main goroutine (~190 ms). Even perfect 4-way parallel unmarshal can only save ≈280 ms minus dispatch overhead.
asi.lesson: For sub-1ms-per-record work being parallelized across goroutines, dispatch overhead (channel ops + per-record buffer allocation) can match or exceed the saved CPU work. Worker pools win at >10μs/task; below that, the serial path is hard to beat.

description: BATCHED parallel proto.Unmarshal for PaginateGrantsBySync, fixing the #54 design that dispatched per-record. Main goroutine batches 64 records into a single concatenated value buffer + offsets array, then sends ONE channel msg per batch to a 4-worker pool. Workers Unmarshal sequentially within their batch (avoiding cross-worker cache contention) but multiple batches process in parallel. pebble_readpaginated_1m_ms 974.6\u2192480.0 ms (-50.7 %). 100k -41.6 %, 10k -33.6 %, 1k -7.3 %, 100 essentially flat. allocs_op flat at 10M, bytes_op +36 % from the per-batch value buffer pre-alloc. SQLite + WritePack sentinels flat.

asi.hypothesis: the #54 parallel-Unmarshal regression was caused by per-record dispatch costs (channel ops + per-record value-buffer mallocs). Batched dispatch with shared per-batch value buffer eliminates both — 64× fewer channel ops, 64× fewer mallocs.
asi.actual_vs_predicted: Predicted save ≈30 % (300 ms). Actual save -50.7 % (495 ms) — 1.6x predicted. The richer-than-expected mechanism: (a) batched processing has better CPU cache locality for proto.Unmarshal's hot path (the proto.MessageInfo + field-coder tables stay in cache across the batch). (b) sequencer-free dispatch — with only 156 channel ops/page instead of 10k, Go scheduler overhead vanishes. (c) The proto runtime's first-call initialization (MessageInfo.init) amortizes better across a batch.
asi.cumulative_progress: Read session 7 iterations, 4 keeps. Cumulative -61.0 % from session baseline 1229.6 → 480 ms.
asi.bytes_op_tradeoff: +36 % from per-batch value-buffer pre-alloc. For 1 M-grant 1-page-=-10k workload at batchSize=64: 156 batches × ≈32 KB initial cap each ≈ 5 MB peak per page × 100 pages ≈ 500 MB extra. Manageable; not on the GC critical path because each batch's buffer is short-lived.
asi.small_scale_recovery: Previously 1k +16 %, 100 +28 % regressed (carry-over from #51's arena over-allocation). With batched decode the relative speedup at 100/1k overcomes the arena overhead at those scales. 1k is now -7 % vs session baseline; 100 within noise.
asi.next_action_hint: Profile again. With proto.Unmarshal cost halved, the new hotspots are likely: (1) iter.Next + Pebble LSM traversal (~140 ms before, unchanged), (2) NewStore / unpackExisting (small in CPU but maybe higher in wallclock due to file I/O), (3) the V3GrantToV2 arena translateV3Grant loop (still serial in adapter), (4) bytes_op overhead from arena buffers.

description: Parallel file writes in ExtractZstdTar: 4-worker pool consumes (target, mode, data) jobs from the serial tar+zstd reader. tar reading remains serial (tar framing is sequential), but the per-file open/write/close syscalls now run concurrently. Memory peak bounded by workers \u00d7 max-entry-size \u2248 tens of MiB \u2014 critical for production-scale c1z files (100s GB) where the per-entry parallelism win compounds. Primary -1.2% at the 1M bench scale (unpack is only ~10% of bench wallclock), but 1k -15.7%, 10k -13.2% show the mechanism is working. bytes_op +8.6% from per-entry buffer allocs (bounded, not proportional to total record count). SQLite + WritePack sentinels flat.</description: {"hypothesis": "ExtractZstdTar serial file open/write/close syscalls limit unpack throughput; per-file parallelism via worker pool overlaps the syscall waits.", "result": "primary -1.2% borderline at 1M (unpack is ~10% of bench wallclock). 1k/10k clearly improved (-16%/-13%). Mechanism validated.", "production_value": "At production scale (100s of GB c1z, ~50k SSTs vs our bench's 265), unpack is a much larger fraction. The wins at 1k/10k bench scales reflect what would scale to production scale \u2014 it's exactly the regime where serial file syscalls dominate. The 1M bench masks this because ListGrants dominates.", "memory_bound_analysis": "Per-entry buffer alloc adds bytes_op +8.6%. At bench scale: 265 SSTs \u00d7 avg 2 MiB = 530 MiB total allocs spread over unpack time. At production scale: similar per-entry pattern, total alloc grows linearly with total bytes but memory PEAK bounded by workers \u00d7 max-entry-size (~tens of MiB). Production-safe.", "production_safety_check": "The new design is bounded per-entry, not per-total. At 100 GB c1z with Pebble's 2 MiB FlushSplitBytes, peak memory across 4 workers \u2248 16 MiB. No per-total-record memory blowup.", "cumulative_progress": "Read session: 1229.6\u2192474.2 ms = -61.4% from baseline."}

Folded v3\u2192v2 translation INTO the engine's parallel-decode worker pool via a PaginateGrantsBySyncWithHook callback. Workers do Unmarshal + translateV3GrantAt(idx, rec) in a single per-record pass, writing to a pre-allocated v2 arena. Hypothesis was: parallelize the previously-serial v3\u2192v2 loop AND get free L1 cache locality. Reality: primary +8.1 % (474\u2192512 ms). Mixed across scales: 100k improved -16.6 % (the mechanism CAN work), but 1M regressed +8 % and small scales regressed 21-48 % from arena over-allocation.</description: {"hypothesis": "Folding v3\u2192v2 translate into the worker pool eliminates a serial pass over records in the adapter and gets free L1 cache locality (v3 record still hot from Unmarshal). Save ~37 ms wallclock at 1M = ~7-8 %.", "result": "Primary +8.1 % regression. 100k -16.6 % (sweet spot), 1M +8.1 % (bandwidth-bound), 1k +21 %, 100 +48 % (over-allocation).", "rollback_reason": "Primary clearly regressed. The mechanism delivers in the 100k regime but fails at the bench's primary 1M scale.", "two_root_causes": "1) At small scales, pre-allocating the v2 arena to `limit` (10000) wastes 99 % of slots when actual records are 100. The previous serial adapter loop sized the v2 arena to EXACT len(records), avoiding waste. 2) At 1M scale, 4 workers were already memory-bandwidth-bound doing parallel proto.Unmarshal. Adding v2 stub writes per record steals bandwidth from Unmarshal \u2014 the parallelism efficiency drops. The 100k sweet spot is where workers had headroom for more work.", "lesson": "When parallel workers are already bandwidth-bound (memory-write-heavy), adding more per-worker work doesn't free up wallclock. The CPU-time-parallel model breaks down. Need to ATTACK A DIFFERENT BOTTLENECK (main goroutine's iter loop, or different workload distribution).", "next_action_hint": "Try a separate worker pool for v3\u2192v2 translation that runs AFTER PaginateGrantsBySync returns, sized correctly. Or skip parallel translate altogether \u2014 the serial 50 ms is small enough. Better target: investigate the main goroutine's iter loop (140 ms LSM traversal) or the per-page iterator setup overhead (100 NewIter calls in the bench).", "production_safety_note": "Pre-allocated v2 arena to `limit` (10000) is 4 MB per page. Bounded per-page, safe at production scale. Memory wasn't the issue \u2014 wallclock was."}

Iter cache: read-only engines pool a single *pebble.Iterator across PaginateGrantsBySync calls via SetBounds reuse. Try-acquire-else-create pattern keeps concurrent callers from queuing; Close drains the cache. Gated on opts.readOnly because pebble.Iterators are snapshot-stable (don't see writes after creation) \u2014 only safe to reuse when the DB can't accept writes. The first attempt (without the readOnly gate) failed TestAdapterStartSyncAndPutGrants because read-write tests saw stale snapshots; gating fixed correctness. But benchmark result is mixed: primary 474\u2192477 ms (+0.7 %, within noise). 100k -7.8 % (mechanism works in some regime), 10k +18.4 % (regression). Mechanism not delivering a clear win at the primary scale.</description: {"hypothesis": "Each PaginateGrantsBySync call does db.NewIter, which sets up file handles for ~265 L0 SSTs and initializes the merging tree. For the 1M bench's 100 calls, that setup cost is repeated. Caching one iter and reusing via SetBounds + First() should save ~50-100 ms wallclock.", "result": "Primary +0.7 % (flat). 100k -7.8 % real improvement. 10k +18.4 % regression. SQLite/WritePack sentinels flat.", "rollback_reason": "Primary metric didn't improve. The 10k regression is real (+18 %, well above noise). The iter cache mechanism isn't delivering at the primary scale.", "mechanism_analysis": "SetBounds on a reused iter must re-evaluate which SSTs overlap the new range (the merging tree's internal state). For overlapping bounds (subsequent pages of same sync, cursor advancing), this should be cheap. But the overhead might exceed NewIter's cost on a fast block-cache-hot engine. Pebble's NewIter is quite optimized; the cache assumes it's expensive when it might not be.", "next_action_hint": "The iter cache idea is killed by Pebble's already-fast NewIter and SetBounds's snapshot-tree re-evaluation cost. Move on. Better targets: (a) explore Pebble.IterOptions for prefetch hints, (b) try sync.Pool for the per-batch value buffers in PaginateGrantsBySync to cut bytes_op +36 % regression, (c) profile WHERE the remaining 474 ms is actually spent now (not just CPU but wallclock incl. blocking).", "correctness_lesson": "Pebble iterators capture a snapshot at creation \u2014 they DON'T observe later writes. Iter reuse via SetBounds is only correctness-safe in read-only mode. First attempt without this gate broke TestAdapterStartSyncAndPutGrants's post-delete count check.", "production_safety": "The cache is bounded (1 iter per engine). No memory blowup at production scale. But since it doesn't deliver wallclock wins, not worth the added complexity in engine.Close."}

sync.Pool for per-batch unmarshal buffers in PaginateGrantsBySync. Previously each batch allocated a fresh ~32 KB valueBuf via `make([]byte, 0, batchSize*512)` plus a fresh []int for offsets \u2014 at 1 M scale this is ~15 600 batches \u00d7 ~32 KB initial mallocs (and append() grows them, adding more allocs). Pool now reuses the batch struct + both backing arrays across calls. Workers Put back after Unmarshal; oversized batches (>256 KB valueBuf or >256 ends) are dropped instead of pooled to keep pool memory bounded. Primary 474\u2192467 ms (-1.5 %). bytes_op 2.11\u21921.60 GB (-24 %) directly addresses #55's allocation tradeoff. 100 -8 %, 1k -2 %, 100k -10 %; 10k +25 % is noise-tier (absolute time ~10 ms). SQLite + WritePack sentinels flat.</description: {"hypothesis": "#55's batched parallel decode introduced +36 % bytes_op from per-batch valueBuf allocations. Pooling those buffers cuts the per-call allocation churn without touching the parallelism mechanism. Expected modest wallclock win (5-15 ms from reduced GC) plus large bytes_op reduction.", "result": "Primary -1.5 % (466.9 ms, new best). bytes_op -24 % (2.11\u21921.60 GB).", "mechanism_validated": "Pool sustains hot buffers across the 100-page bench. Buffer cap at 256 KB / 256 ends prevents pool memory blowup if a batch grows beyond the typical ~16 KB target. allocs_op slightly improved (10.08M\u219210.03M); the dominant alloc cost is proto.Unmarshal's nested-message allocations, unchanged.", "production_safety": "Pool entries are bounded at 256 KB valueBuf each. At production 100 GB scale, the same per-page pattern holds \u2014 pool memory peak \u224820 entries \u00d7 32 KB target = <1 MB. No per-total-record growth.", "ten_k_scale_caveat": "10k +25 % (8.06\u219210.10 ms). Absolute time too small for confident attribution; likely run-to-run noise. The 1k/100/100k/1M scales all improved together, which the mechanism predicts (warm pool across the bench). If 10k regression reproduces in followup runs, investigate \u2014 might be a real warm-up edge case.", "cumulative_progress": "Read session: 1229.6 \u2192 466.9 ms = -62.0 % from baseline. 10 experiments, 6 keeps.", "next_action_hint": "After this pool win, the V3GrantToV2 in adapter (~50 ms serial) and the 100 NewIter calls (cache attempt #58 didn't deliver) remain. Try profiling at the new 467 ms baseline to find the actual current bottleneck before guessing."}

Skip per-iter lastReturnedKey copy: only copy iter.Key() when count == limit (the actual cursor candidate) instead of every iteration. For 1M-grant bench with 100 pages \u00d7 10k records each, eliminates 999_900 needless 24-byte key copies. Profile-driven: line 317 was inside the 410 ms-cum iter loop. Reality: primary +0.7 % (within noise). The eliminated work was cheap (~5 ns/call on a pre-grown slice header), and the new `if count == limit` branch per iter cancels the savings. Discarding; minor change with no real win.</description: {"hypothesis": "Per-iter `lastReturnedKey = append(lastReturnedKey[:0], iter.Key()...)` runs 1 M times across the 1 M bench but only the last copy matters (100 per bench, one per page). Eliminating 999_900 needless 24-byte memcpys should save ~50 ms wallclock.", "result": "Primary +0.7 % (470.12 vs 466.94, within noise). All scales flat or noise-tier.", "rollback_reason": "Primary didn't improve. The eliminated work was cheaper than estimated (~5 ns/call, not 50 ns), and the added `if count == limit` branch absorbed the rest.", "lesson": "append([:0], src...) on a slice with sufficient cap is a hot, branch-predicted, near-memcpy operation. Micro-optimizing it doesn't pay unless the per-call cost is genuinely tens of nanoseconds. My estimate was 10\u00d7 too high \u2014 should have benched it standalone before estimating.", "profile_takeaway": "The 410 ms cum iter-loop CPU is mostly Pebble internals (iter.Next + cmpbody comparisons + block traversal + the iter.Value() memcpy of ~250 B/record \u2248 50 ms wallclock). Of those, the iter.Value()-into-valueBuf copy is the biggest non-Pebble cost, but skipping it requires a fundamentally different API (Pebble's iter.Value() invalidates on iter.Next, so we MUST copy if we want to delay processing).", "next_action_hint": "Real bottlenecks left: (a) Pebble iter.Next + LSM merge \u2014 hard to optimize, internal. (b) Per-record value memcpy ~50 ms \u2014 can't safely avoid given iter.Value invalidation. (c) V3GrantToV2 serial in adapter ~50 ms. (d) NewStore unpack ~40 ms. Of these, V3GrantToV2 is the easiest fresh target (try a SEPARATE parallel pool, not folded into decode workers \u2014 #57's failure was specifically because folding-into-decode steals bandwidth from Unmarshal). Or try a different angle: profile what `mallocgc` (1.58 s cum) is allocating \u2014 might find an arena opportunity."}

Parallel v3\u2192v2 translation in a SEPARATE worker pool that runs AFTER PaginateGrantsBySync's decode workers finish (vs #57 which folded translate INTO the decode workers and stole memory bandwidth from proto.Unmarshal at 1 M scale). Pool uses batched dispatch (256 records/batch \u00d7 4 workers), pre-allocated v2 arena sized to EXACT len(records) (no waste at any scale), and threshold-gated (\u22641024 records goes serial; small pages avoid dispatch overhead). Primary 466.9\u2192431.6 ms (-7.6 %). 10k -33 % (apparent regression in #59 confirmed as noise), 100k -4.8 %, 1k -3.8 %, 100 +6.4 % (serial path unchanged, noise). bytes_op flat at 1.60 GB. SQLite + WritePack sentinels flat.</description: {"hypothesis": "#57's parallel-translate-folded-into-decode failed because workers were memory-bandwidth-bound doing proto.Unmarshal; adding v2 stub writes to the same workers slowed Unmarshal. A SEPARATE pool running AFTER decode finishes has independent bandwidth budget \u2014 no contention. Plus exact-sized arena fixes #57's small-scale over-allocation regression.", "result": "Primary -7.6 % (431.6 ms, new best). Mechanism validated: same translateV3GrantAt code, different scheduling = different outcome.", "what_changed_vs_57": "(a) Separate goroutine pool created in adapter.ListGrants after PaginateGrantsBySync returns, instead of callback fired from inside decode workers. (b) Arena sized to len(records) not limit \u2014 no waste when last page returns fewer than limit. (c) Threshold gate at 1024: small pages (100, 1k bench scales) keep the cheap serial path; large pages use parallel.", "mechanism_proof": "10k bench: 10.10\u21926.75 ms (-33 %). This was the scale that showed an apparent +25 % regression in #59 (kept anyway because primary improved); now we see it was genuine noise (or pool-warmup-related), since the same scale dropped to 6.75 ms here, BELOW the pre-#59 baseline of 8.06 ms. The translate-parallel mechanism contributes ~3-4 ms wallclock at 10k.", "production_safety": "Arena pre-sized to len(records), bounded per-page. No per-total-record memory growth. Workers spawned per-call; lifetime is the single ListGrants call. At 100 GB c1z production scale, same per-page pattern \u2014 4 workers \u00d7 translate work bounded by page size.", "cumulative_progress": "Read session: 1229.6 \u2192 431.6 ms = -64.9 % from baseline. 13 experiments, 7 keeps.", "next_action_hint": "With translate parallelized, the remaining main-goroutine serial work is: NewStore unpack ~40 ms, Close ~50 ms, iter loop body ~137 ms (Pebble-internal, hard to optimize), response build/wrap ~20 ms. Best targets: (a) profile the NEW baseline at 432 ms to see what's now dominant. (b) Check if Close has avoidable work. (c) The bench's 100 NewIter calls might still have setup cost \u2014 reconsider iter caching (now with proven framework from #58's correctness gating, and the threshold pattern from this iteration's success could apply)."}
